directions.name: Keep The Accepted PTX Branch And Make The Steady-State Sequence More Explicit
directions.hypothesis: The round-40 runtime regression should not be treated as a branch failure. The active PTX hot-band call site in commit 09d3544 is behaviorally identical to the round-38 new-best commit e26d834, and the measured stall mix is effectively the same: tensor-active 47.84 vs 47.87, warps-active 16.51 vs 16.51, barrier 10.96 vs 11.13, short_scoreboard 5.88 vs 5.87, and mio_throttle 0.57 vs 0.55. That means the accepted full B-reuse PTX branch is still the right base. The next move should keep the current two-stage cp.async pipeline, the padded export scratch, and the accepted consumer-side B-reuse pattern intact, but rewrite the active 128x128 K16 PTX hot loop into a more explicit prologue / steady-state / epilogue sequence so the branch pays less control-flow and synchronization overhead per K tile. For this round the primary human-idea family is Stage plus Async Copy plus Pg2s, but only as sequencing cleanup on top of the already-working baseline rather than a deeper-stage rewrite.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Synchronization and orchestration overhead in the active PTX steady-state loop. The accepted branch has already collapsed mio_throttle, so the next runtime limiter is the combination of CTA barrier cost and warp-local short scoreboard that comes from the current wait / sync / recursive consume sequence.
directions.risk: Medium. The safest version keeps tile shape, stage depth, and the accepted B-reuse dataflow fixed, but even a sequencing-only cleanup can accidentally reintroduce extra waits, raise register lifetime, or disturb the low-mio behavior that currently makes the PTX branch competitive.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the sequencing rewrite does not improve barrier or short_scoreboard without materially raising mio_throttle, or if runtime still cannot beat the accepted 25.974272 ms branch after one focused round.

directions.name: Keep The Accepted PTX Branch And Refine The Export Scratch Path
directions.hypothesis: The accepted branch already combined the full B-reuse helper with the padded PTX export scratch, and that pairing is still the best measured configuration. Since round-40 is measurement noise rather than a real branch regression, the export path remains a legitimate next family on top of the accepted branch. The PTX export helpers still round-trip accumulator tiles through two padded 20-float shared-memory rows per stage, then reconstruct quad stores row by row. A more careful export-side refinement could preserve the accepted low-mio hot loop while shaving shared-bank traffic, quad unpack work, or post-loop barrier cost. The primary human-idea family here is Bank Conflict, with Data Reuse and Coalescing Access treated as supporting details rather than the main ranking driver.
directions.expected_bottleneck: c_shared round-trip and shared-memory export overhead after the hot loop, especially bank writes, LSU wavefront pressure, and paired stage bookkeeping in the PTX-only export helpers.
directions.risk: Medium. The export scratch is correctness-sensitive and tightly coupled to shared-memory budgeting. Over-tightening padding, reducing stage pairing too aggressively, or changing store packing can reintroduce bank conflicts or alignment problems and erase the round-38 gain.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if export-path edits fail to improve runtime or fail to improve the shared-bank and LSU metrics they are targeting on top of the accepted branch.

directions.name: Keep The Accepted B-Reuse Pattern But Narrow The Consumer-Side Delivery Tweak
directions.hypothesis: The accepted PTX branch proved that consumer-side B reuse is directionally correct because it held mio_throttle near 0.55%, but the remaining short_scoreboard and barrier cost says the current warp-local delivery pattern is not yet ideal. A bounded follow-up should preserve the accepted branch's core idea of explicit consumer-side B delivery and avoid any CTA repack, deeper staging, or full fallback to the baseline helper. The bounded tweak would target only the warp-local consume pattern: mirrored-column order, fragment live range, lane remap, or a narrower reuse scope that keeps the accepted low-mio signal while trying to claw back some of the remaining short_scoreboard cost. The primary human-idea families here are Register Reuse plus Ps2r plus Bank Conflict.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Warp-local B-fragment delivery and register residency inside the active PTX microkernel. The current branch already solved the old mio problem, so the question is whether a narrower consumer-side variant can keep that win while reducing scoreboard pressure.
directions.risk: Medium-high. This is the easiest direction to slide back into the already-weaker baseline helper shape or to trade away the accepted low-mio behavior for only a cosmetic scoreboard improvement. It is ranked third because the accepted branch already demonstrated the right consumer-side family, so the next round should first attack steady-state orchestration before touching the warp-local reuse pattern again.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if the bounded consumer-side tweak pushes mio_throttle back toward the old 4% to 5% range, raises register pressure, or fails to beat the accepted branch after one isolated round.

notes: Round 41/100 should stay centered on the accepted round-38 PTX hot-band branch, not on the noisy 27.088384 ms measurement alone. The main-agent comparison between commit e26d834 and commit 09d3544 found no behavioral source change at the active PTX microkernel call site, only formatting, and the headline NCU metrics are effectively unchanged. That makes round 40 a run-to-run variance event, not evidence that the round-38 branch was wrong. Human-idea audit for this round: Tiling is rejected as the primary move because the real active 256x128 CTA / 64x64 warp promotion already regressed badly on the default path; Coalescing Access is accepted as already implemented through wide 16-byte async copy and does not explain the remaining gap; Data Reuse is accepted and stays fundamental because the active branch's win still depends on explicit warp-local B reuse; Async Copy is accepted only as the current working baseline, while deeper staging is deferred because earlier heavier pipeline changes regressed; Bank Conflict is accepted and remains important through both the padded export scratch and any future consumer-side lane mapping cleanup; L2 Cache is deferred because grouped ordering helped an older branch but does not explain the accepted PTX branch's current residual stalls; Register Reuse is accepted and remains a core lever on the consumer side; Pg2s is accepted as already present in the two-stage cp.async pipeline; Ps2r is accepted and still relevant for warp-local B delivery; Stage is accepted only in the narrower sense of steady-state sequencing cleanup, while deeper or more complex staging is rejected for this round. The ranking is still aimed at the real target of sub-20 ms, not merely at edging out the 25.917889 ms CUTLASS baseline.
